feature_name: Dual-mode operation (Root Complex and End Point)
description: The controller supports dual operation as a Root Complex (RC) or as an End Point (EP). Bootstraps PCIESSMODE[1:0] select the default mode and software can override it via device registers. In RC mode, it initiates configuration, memory, and I/O transactions to downstream devices. In EP mode, it exposes BARs, responds to configuration, and can originate bus-master traffic when enabled. This flexibility allows the same silicon to serve host or device roles in PCIe systems.
dependencies: DEVSTAT.PCIESSMODE, CMD_STATUS.LTSSM_EN
Remarks: Choose RC for host-side enumeration and EP for device-side integration; ensure software aligns initialization sequences with selected role.

feature_name: x1/x2 link, Gen1/Gen2 speeds, dynamic width, lane reversal, polarity inversion
description: The PCIe link supports 1- or 2-lane operation with automatic lane reversal and polarity inversion on receive. It operates at 2.5 GT/s (Gen1) and 5.0 GT/s (Gen2) with ultra-low TX/RX latency. Dynamic-width conversion is supported during link training to maintain connectivity. Link speed change to Gen2 is driven by LTSSM using directed speed control bits. These features improve interoperability across diverse link partners and routing constraints.
dependencies: PL_LINK_CTRL.LNK_MODE, LINK_CTRL2.TGT_SPEED/COMMON_CLK_CFG, PL_GEN2.DIR_SPD/LN_EN, SerDes polarity fields
Remarks: Start links in Gen1, then enable directed speed up to Gen2 when stability is confirmed; leave lane reversal/polarity auto unless board reversals exist.

feature_name: Outbound address translation (32 regions)
description: Outbound translator maps internal bus addresses to PCIe addresses across 32 equally sized regions. Region sizes of 1/2/4/8 MB are set by OB_SIZE and each region uses OB_OFFSET_INDEXn/OB_OFFSETn_HI with enable. It supports 32- or 64-bit PCIe addressing and adds the lower address offset automatically. Transactions violating region boundaries have unspecified behavior. This enables clean memory windows from local masters to remote PCIe targets.
dependencies: CMD_STATUS.OB_XLT_EN, OB_SIZE, OB_OFFSET_INDEXn, OB_OFFSETn_HI
Remarks: Align buffers so bursts do not cross translation window boundaries; select larger regions when using large DMA bursts.

feature_name: Inbound address translation (4 regions) and BAR mapping
description: Inbound translator remaps accepted PCIe accesses to the device's memory map using four regions. Regions are associated with BARs via IB_BARn and use IB_STARTn_{HI,LO} and IB_OFFSETn for the mapping. BAR0 is an exception and always maps to application registers for both RC and EP, and cannot be remapped. In 64-bit modes, adjacent BARs pair to hold the full address. This enables precise placement of inbound traffic into on-chip memory or peripherals.
dependencies: CMD_STATUS.IB_XLT_EN, IB_BARn, IB_STARTn_{HI,LO}, IB_OFFSETn, BARx in config space
Remarks: Associate IB regions only with data BARs (not BAR0); verify that target memory is non-cacheable and 128B-aligned for best throughput.

feature_name: Configurable BAR sizing via BAR Mask registers and DBI_CS2
description: BAR Mask registers overlay on BARx to control the size and enable state visible to RC during enumeration. Software enables writes to masks by setting CMD_STATUS.DBI_CS2, programs masks, then clears DBI_CS2 before enumeration. BAR0 mask is fixed to the application-register window constraints. This allows an EP to request the desired aperture sizes and types from the RC. Mask registers are write-only and validated by test patterns on the BARs.
dependencies: CMD_STATUS.DBI_CS2, BARx_MASK, BARx
Remarks: Always clear DBI_CS2 before handing control to RC; never modify masks from the serial link side.

feature_name: Endian support and byte swapping
description: The subsystem supports both little and big endian systems via an ENDIAN mode register. For inbound and outbound transactions in big endian mode, selectable swap granularities (1/2/4/8-byte) are applied as per tables. Little endian operation requires no swapping. TLP FBE/LBE handling cooperates with the swap mode to preserve intended byte enables. This allows seamless integration in big-endian SoCs.
dependencies: ENDIAN[1:0] selection, system endianness strap
Remarks: Choose swap granularity to match bus access widths; use 4-byte swap for 32-bit MMRs and 1-byte for generic buffers unless profiling dictates otherwise.

feature_name: MSI and Legacy interrupt generation (EP) and reception (RC)
description: As an EP, the module can generate Legacy INTA/B/C/D messages or MSI writes to an RC-provided address/data. MSI supports up to 32 vectors exposed via MSIn_* register banks. As an RC, it receives both Legacy and MSI interrupts and maps them to CPU events. MSI_IRQ register mediates vector-triggered interrupts from remote devices. This provides flexible interrupt signaling across endpoints and host.
dependencies: MSI_CAP/MSI_LOW32/MSI_UP32/MSI_DATA, MSI_IRQ, MSIn_IRQ_* enables/status, EP_IRQ_SET/CLR, IRQ_EOI
Remarks: Prefer MSI over Legacy for scalability; clear MSIn status and write IRQ_EOI after servicing to allow re-trigger.

feature_name: Interrupt allocation and EOI handling
description: The PCIESS maps a total of 14 grouped interrupts to the device interrupt controller, covering Legacy, MSI groups, error, power/reset, and link events. Each event group has raw/enabled status and enable/clear registers. End-of-interrupt is acknowledged by writing the event number into IRQ_EOI. Some races can occur with Legacy deasserts; software should probe downstream status before EOI. This grouping simplifies CPU-side vector management.
dependencies: IRQ_EOI, LEGACY_* and MSI* IRQ registers, ERR/PMRST IRQ blocks
Remarks: Mask only necessary sources; for Legacy in RC, read device interrupt cause before issuing EOI to avoid retrigger.

feature_name: Device and Link power management (D-states, ASPM L0s/L1)
description: The device supports PCIe device power states D0–D3 and link power states L0/L0s/L1 with software and ASPM control. L2/L3 are not supported in the link; D3cold requires external power conditions and is not retained without Vaux. PMCMD/PMCFG manage PME messaging and entry readiness, and LINK_STAT_CTRL controls ASPM. Hardware can autonomously manage transitions for power savings. This enables fine-grained link and device energy control.
dependencies: ASPM enable in LINK_STAT_CTRL.ACTIVE_LINK_PM, PMCTL/PMCFG, CLK sharing config
Remarks: Enable ASPM only when both partners support it and latency tolerance is acceptable; consider disabling in high-throughput/low-latency paths.

feature_name: Advanced error reporting (AER) and baseline error handling
description: The controller implements PCIe baseline error reporting and extended AER. Status, mask, and severity registers exist for correctable and uncorrectable classes, and Root Complex tracking is supported with ROOT_ERR_* and ERR_SRC_ID. ECRC generation/check can be enabled where system devices support it. Header log registers capture failing TLP headers for diagnosis. This framework enables robust detection, logging, and escalation of link and transaction errors.
evidence: Sections 2.16.1–2.16.2.3; Registers 3.8.x (PCIE_UNCERR*, PCIE_CERR*, PCIE_ACCR, ROOT_ERR_*, ERR_SRC_ID)
dependencies: Enable bits in DEV_STAT_CTRL, PCIE_CERR_MASK/UNCERR_MASK/SVRTY, PCIE_ACCR.ECRC_*
Remarks: Disable ECRC if link partner does not support it (PCIE_ACCR note); tune severities to system policy and enable Root error interrupts as needed.

feature_name: PIPE and PHY loopback modes
description: Two loopback mechanisms are provided: link-layer (PIPE) and PHY loopback. PIPE loopback supports master/slave roles for symbol-level testing with test equipment. PHY loopback enables end-to-end TLP loopback in RC mode without a link partner using SerDes loopback bits. PL_LINK_CTRL.LPBK_EN and SERDES_CFGx control enablement and PL_FORCE_LINK can force LTSSM states. These modes aid bring-up and margining.
dependencies: SerDes PLL lock and LINK training disabled/enabled in sequence; PL_FORCE_LINK to POLL_ACTIVE for PHY loopback
Remarks: Configure loopback before sending traffic to avoid sequence mismatch; restrict to lab/test flows and restore normal control afterward.

feature_name: Clocking and SerDes PLL configuration
description: The PHY requires a differential REFCLK (commonly 100 MHz) and an internal PLL (PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL) to generate line rate clocking. Software must enable ENPLL and wait for LOCK in PCIE_SERDES_STS before any PCIe transactions. Common-clock and SSC topologies are supported, with COMMON_CLK_CFG indicating shared clock. Correct LB/MPY settings are needed for 2.5/5.0 GT/s operation. Stable clocking is foundational for link reliability.
dependencies: Provide compliant REFCLK, program CFGPLL (LB/MPY/ENPLL), poll STS.LOCK, then enable LTSSM_EN
Remarks: With SSC backplanes use common clock architecture; do not access PCIe MMRs before PLL lock.

feature_name: Reset mechanisms (hot reset, LTSSM control, power domain)
description: Hardware and software resets are supported, including hot reset issuance by writing RSTCMD.INIT_RST. LTSSM enable (CMD_STATUS.LTSSM_EN) gates link training, and post-reset delays (≥100 ms) are required before transactions. Power domain off/on resets PCIe state and requires link re-initialization. Proper sequencing prevents hangs and ensures clean recovery. This supports robust error recovery and reconfiguration workflows.
dependencies: RSTCMD.INIT_RST, CMD_STATUS.LTSSM_EN, PSC domain control
Remarks: Quiesce traffic and confirm buffers empty (ACT_STATUS/FLUSH_N) before reset; re-run initialization and enumeration after power cycling.

feature_name: Address Space 0 (local app regs, local/remote config, remote IO window)
description: A 16 KB Address Space 0 is partitioned into four 4 KB regions for application registers, local PCIe configuration, remote configuration, and (RC-only) a 4 KB IO window. Remote config/IO spaces are directly memory-mapped and must not be accessed when the link is down. Local application registers are also reachable via BAR0 from the link side. This structured map simplifies software access paths for control and enumeration.
dependencies: BAR0 mapping to app regs; CFG_SETUP for remote config; IOBASE for IO window base
Remarks: Gate remote windows on link-up status; issue only single 32-bit, non-burst accesses to these windows as specified.

feature_name: TLP attribute controls: Relaxed Ordering and No Snoop
description: Global attributes for outgoing TLPs are programmable in TLPCFG (RELAXED and NO_SNOOP). Device Control also exposes per-function controls for error report enables and ordering. Relaxed ordering can improve throughput but may reorder posted writes versus MSIs. Disabling relaxed ordering may be necessary to preserve producer-consumer semantics. This control allows balancing performance and ordering guarantees.
dependencies: TLPCFG.RELAXED/NO_SNOOP, DEV_STAT_CTRL.RELAXED
Remarks: If MSI is used to signal data readiness, consider clearing RELAXED to prevent race between data and interrupt arrival.

feature_name: Performance tuning: speed change, FTS/ACK frequency, timers
description: Several knobs exist to tune link performance and stability. PL_GEN2.DIR_SPD and LINK_CTRL2.TGT_SPEED manage Gen1→Gen2 transitions, while NUM_FTS and ACK_FREQ influence training and ACK DLLP cadence. SYM_NUM exposes timer modifiers for replay and ACK/Nak latency, and ACK_FREQ sets ASPM entrance latencies and common/NFTS counts. Proper configuration helps optimize throughput without sacrificing reliability. Adjustments should be validated against link partners.
evidence: Sections 2.3.3 note; 2.10.1; Registers 3.9.13 PL_GEN2, 3.7.13 LINK_CTRL2, 3.9.5 ACK_FREQ, 3.9.8 SYM_NUM
dependencies: PL_GEN2.NUM_FTS/DIR_SPD, LINK_CTRL2 fields, ACK_FREQ.* and SYM_NUM.*
Remarks: Use defaults for initial bring-up; increase ACK frequency only if interoperability is confirmed; document any deviations for compliance.

feature_name: Diagnostics and debug (LTSSM state, error injection, activity)
description: DEBUG0/DEBUG1 provide visibility into LTSSM state, TS sequences, electrical idle, polarity tests, and training status. DIAG_CTRL allows one-shot inversion of LCRC/ECRC for error-injection testing. ACT_STATUS exposes inbound/outbound buffer non-empty indicators to aid quiesce decisions. Appendix A maps LTSSM encoded values to human-readable states. These capabilities accelerate bring-up and issue triage.
evidence: Registers 3.9.11 DEBUG0, 3.9.12 DEBUG1, 3.1.12 DIAG_CTRL, 3.1.10 ACT_STATUS; Appendix A
dependencies: Readable DEBUG registers; DIAG_CTRL INV_LCRC/INV_ECRC; ACT_STATUS bits
Remarks: Log LTSSM state transitions during link issues; clear error-injection bits only after observing expected faults on analyzers.

feature_name: DMA enablement via internal master/slave ports and EDMA examples
description: The PCIESS lacks a built-in DMA engine but exposes a 128-bit slave port for outbound (local master→PCIe) and a 128-bit master port for inbound (PCIe→local) transactions. External DMA (e.g., EDMA) can saturate the link, with guidance showing payload sizing and indexed transfers. Master port can also initiate transactions on behalf of remote requesters. This architecture allows flexible, high-throughput data movement.
dependencies: System EDMA, OB/IB translation enabled, bus mastering (STATUS_COMMAND.BUS_MS)
Remarks: Size bursts to 128B and align to 8B; compute offsets carefully for BAR/translation windows as in examples.

feature_name: Ingress filtering and UR behavior control (security/hardening)
description: Filter masks allow dropping or accepting certain TLP classes (CFG/IO/MSG) on RC and controlling treatment of malformed or out-of-BAR accesses. SYMTIMER_FLTMASK fields can force UR on poisoned or function-mismatched TLPs, or allow them for debug. Additional controls disable DLLP aborts or vendor messages if desired. These options help harden the ingress path against unexpected traffic. Configuration must match system trust assumptions.
dependencies: SYMTIMER_FLTMASK.F1_* fields, FLT_MASK2.*
Remarks: Default to strict UR for out-of-BAR/poisoned/function-mismatch; only relax for controlled lab tests or interop experiments.

feature_name: Configuration access engine (RC) and IO window (RC-only)
description: As an RC, software can issue configuration Type 0/1 reads/writes to downstream devices by programming CFG_SETUP and then accessing the remote configuration window. A 4 KB IO window is exposed in Address Space 0, with IOBASE supplying the base for outbound IO TLPs. Only non-burst, 32-bit accesses are allowed for these windows. This mechanism supports enumeration and legacy IO control where required.
dependencies: CFG_SETUP (bus/dev/func/type), IOBASE, TLPCFG attributes
Remarks: Validate link-up and bus numbers before remote config; avoid inbound IO (not supported) and respect 32-bit single-access rule.

feature_name: Transaction rules: alignment, burst type, byte strobes
description: Internal interface requires transactions ≤128 bytes and 8-byte alignment reduces max length to 120 bytes if misaligned. Only incremental bursts are supported; fixed/wrap bursts lead to unspecified behavior. Byte enables must be a contiguous string of ones without gaps across the write range. Adhering to these rules ensures reliable bridging between PCIe TLPs and the internal bus. Violations can cause splits or undefined behavior.
dependencies: EDMA or software generators must shape bursts accordingly
Remarks: Pad and align buffers to 128B boundaries; avoid holey byte enables; expect split reads at 128B boundaries on inbound.

feature_name: BAR0 application register mapping (RC/EP)
description: BAR0 is permanently reserved to map the PCIESS application registers from the link side in both RC and EP roles. This mapping enables RC software to control or monitor an EP even without on-device firmware. Accesses to BAR0 cannot be disabled or remapped elsewhere. The consistent addressability simplifies tooling and debug integration. This behavior is foundational to MSI register access conventions as well.
dependencies: BAR0 fixed mapping; EP/RC config spaces
Remarks: Protect BAR0 aperture appropriately in host software; avoid exposing sensitive knobs beyond trusted domains.
